op.name: nested_iters
op.notes: Create nested iterators for multi-index broadcasting. Not supported.
op.summary: Create nditers for use in nested loops
op.signature: we.nested_iters(op, axes, flags=None, op_flags=None, op_dtypes=None,     order="K", casting="safe", buffersize=0)

blocks.text: Create nditers for use in nested loops

Create a tuple of `nditer` objects which iterate in nested loops over
different axes of the op argument. The first iterator is used in the
outermost loop, the last in the innermost loop. Advancing one will change
the subsequent iterators to point at its new element.

desc_blocks.text: Each item is used as an "op_axes" argument to an nditer

items.name: flags, op_flags, op_dtypes, order, casting, buffersize (optional)

desc_blocks.text: See `nditer` parameters of the same name
An nditer for each item in `axes`, outermost first

blocks.text:
Basic usage. Note how y is the "flattened" version of
[a[:, 0, :], a[:, 1, 0], a[:, 2, :]] since we specified
the first iter's axes as [1]

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.arange(12).reshape(2, 3, 2)
>>> i, j = np.nested_iters(a, [[1], [0, 2]], flags=["multi_index"])
>>> for x in i:
...      print(i.multi_index)
...      for y in j:
...          print('', j.multi_index, y)
(0,)
 (0, 0) 0
 (0, 1) 1
 (1, 0) 6
 (1, 1) 7
(1,)
 (0, 0) 2
 (0, 1) 3
 (1, 0) 8
 (1, 1) 9
(2,)
 (0, 0) 4
 (0, 1) 5
 (1, 0) 10
 (1, 1) 11
